Conclusion: The paper establishes a principled and efficient framework for uncertainty quantification in LLM evaluations and inference from pairwise data, offering theoretical advancements and practical tools for related applications in low-rank structures.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) evaluation platforms increasingly rely on pairwise human judgments. These data are noisy, sparse, and non-uniform, yet leaderboards are reported with limited uncertainty quantification. We study this as semiparametric inference for a low-rank latent score tensor observed through pairwise comparisons under Bradley-Terry-Luce-type models. This places LLM evaluation in a new tensor completion setting with structured observations, non-uniform sampling, and pairwise contrasts. Our target is a smooth functional $ψ(T^\star)$, including linear estimands such as ability gaps and nonlinear ones such as win probabilities. We derive the information operator on the low-rank tangent space, the efficient influence function, and the semiparametric efficiency bound, then construct a one-step debiased estimator with asymptotic normality. A central challenge is that the information operator is anisotropic and does not commute with the tangent-space projection, creating a bottleneck absent from isotropic models. We introduce a score-whitening method that equalizes local Fisher information and restores stable inference at the optimal sample-complexity scale. Our results provide a principled framework for uncertainty quantification in LLM evaluation and more broadly for inference on low-rank structures from pairwise data.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [502] [Symetra: Visual Analytics for the Parameter Tuning Process of Symbolic Execution Engines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05349)
*Donghee Hong,Minjong Kim,Sooyoung Cha,Jaemin Jo*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper introduces Symetra, a visual analytics tool to aid users in optimizing parameters for symbolic execution engines like KLEE, improving branch coverage and efficiency through Human-in-the-Loop processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing symbolic execution engines struggle with numerous parameters which are hard to tune, leading users to depend on suboptimal default configurations. Automated tuners do not provide sufficient insight into why certain configurations work well, thus prompting the need for Human-in-the-Loop methods.

Method: The authors developed Symetra, a visual analytics system that provides overviews of parameter impacts and patterns, and allows users to conduct collective analyses to contrast configurations and identify impactful differences.

Result: Case studies demonstrated that Symetra enabled experts to interpret parameter impacts effectively, identify complementary configurations, and outperform automated tuners in both branch coverage and efficiency.

Conclusion: Human-in-the-Loop approaches via tools like Symetra are beneficial in symbolic execution engine tuning, offering insights and improving performance beyond automated solutions.

Abstract: Symbolic execution engines such as KLEE automatically generate test cases to maximize branch coverage, but their numerous parameters make it difficult to understand the parameters' impact, leading the user to rely on suboptimal default configurations. While automated tuners have shown promising results, they provide limited insights into why certain configurations work well, motivating the need for Human-in-the-Loop approaches. In this work, we present a visual analytics system, Symetra, designed to support Human-in-the-Loop parameter tuning of symbolic execution engines. To handle a large number of parameters and their configurations, we provide two complementary overviews of their impact on branch coverage values and patterns. Building on these overviews, our system enables collective analysis, allowing the user to contrast groups of configurations and identify differences that may affect branch coverage. We also report on case studies and a Human-in-the-Loop tuning process, demonstrating that experts not only interpreted parameter impacts and identified complementary configurations, but also improved upon fully automated approaches in both branch coverage and tuning efficiency.

</details>


### [503] [WSCM-Lite: A Practitioner-Ready Implementation of the Weak Signal Cultivation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05381)
*Maurice Codourey,Emmanuel A. Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces WSCM-Lite, a simpler lookup-table version of the Weak Signal Cultivation Model (WSCM) for tracking risks, eliminating complexities like exponential functions while closely approximating the original model's results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to enable organizations with limited computational infrastructure (e.g., Excel users) to adopt the WSCM framework for tracking and identifying risk signals by simplifying its technical requirements.

Method: The authors simplify WSCM by replacing its continuous recency weighting, momentum, and complex parameters with a lookup-table approach using seven formulas and five constants. They validate the model's accuracy and behavior through worked examples, boundary cases, and sensitivity analyses.

Result: WSCM-Lite closely replicates WSCM's trajectory within a 0.01 field unit margin and demonstrates robustness to gap threshold variations. A worked example shows it performs comparably to WSCM, identifying key signal regions and escalations with minimal lag.

Conclusion: WSCM-Lite offers organizations an accessible, Excel-compatible alternative to the full WSCM model, maintaining accuracy and stability while significantly reducing computational complexity.

Abstract: The Weak Signal Cultivation Model (WSCM) provides a mathematically rigorous framework for tracking frontline risk signals across a two-dimensional coordinate field using 15 equations and 16 tunable parameters. While this specification is designed for eventual software implementation, its computational requirements create an adoption barrier for organizations whose available infrastructure is a spreadsheet. This paper introduces WSCM-Lite, a lookup-table implementation that reproduces the full WSCM's coordinate trajectories within 0.01 field units while eliminating all exponential functions, state-dependent tracking, and free parameters. The simplification replaces continuous recency weighting with a four-row lookup table and removes consensus momentum and reversal amplification entirely, reducing the specification to seven formulas and five hardcoded constants. A 26-session worked example using the Gas Fumes signal from the parent paper demonstrates that WSCM-Lite traverses the same four-region path (Question Marks --> Lit Fuses --> Owls --> Sleeping Cats --> Question Marks) and triggers SMS escalation within two sessions of the full model. Five additional scenarios validate boundary behavior, and a sensitivity analysis confirms stability under +/-30% gap threshold variation. An accompanying Excel simulator and supplementary materials are publicly available at https://github.com/emmgonai/wscm-lite.

</details>


### [504] [From Use to Oversight: How Mental Models Influence User Behavior and Output in AI Writing Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05166)
*Shalaleh Rismani,Su Lin Blodgett,Q. Vera Liao,Alexandra Olteanu,AJung Moon*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper investigates how users' mental models of AI writing assistants affect their usage and writing outcomes, revealing that structural understanding of the system can lead to increased trust but more grammatical errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how users’ mental models shape their interaction and oversight of AI-based writing assistants, particularly in contexts where the system can generate errors.

Method: Priming participants with two types of mental models (functional and structural), using a writing assistant for a cover letter task which occasionally offered ungrammatical suggestions, and analyzing their control behavior and outcomes.

Result: Participants with a structural mental model demonstrated better understanding and judged the system as more usable but produced more error-prone outputs, showing a complex relationship between system knowledge, trust, and oversight.

Conclusion: Improved structural understanding of AI systems may enhance perceived usability but can also undermine critical oversight, suggesting a need for balance between user trust and system error management.

Abstract: AI-based writing assistants are ubiquitous, yet little is known about how users' mental models shape their use. We examine two types of mental models -- functional or related to what the system does, and structural or related to how the system works -- and how they affect control behavior -- how users request, accept, or edit AI suggestions as they write -- and writing outcomes. We primed participants ($N = 48$) with different system descriptions to induce these mental models before asking them to complete a cover letter writing task using a writing assistant that occasionally offered preconfigured ungrammatical suggestions to test whether the mental models affected participants' critical oversight. We find that while participants in the structural mental model condition demonstrate a better understanding of the system, this can have a backfiring effect: while these participants judged the system as more usable, they also produced letters with more grammatical errors, highlighting a complex relationship between system understanding, trust, and control in contexts that require user oversight of error-prone AI outputs.

</details>


### [505] [OGA-AID: Clinician-in-the-loop AI Report Drafting Assistant for Multimodal Observational Gait Analysis in Post-Stroke Rehabilitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05360)
*Khoi T. N. Nguyen,Nghia D. Nguyen,Hui Yu Koh,Patrick W. H. Kwong,Karen Sui Geok Chua,Ananda Sidarta,Baosheng Yu*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces OGA-AID, a multi-agent AI system aiding clinicians in drafting multimodal gait assessment reports that combines patient data with improved accuracy and reduced error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenges clinicians face in integrating gait videos and motion-capture data into structured rehabilitation reports, which is both time-intensive and cognitively demanding.

Method: OGA-AID uses a system of 3 specialized agents to synthesize patient movement recordings, kinematic data, and clinical profiles into structured assessments. It further incorporates clinician-in-the-loop settings, allowing experts to provide preliminary notes that enhance system accuracy.

Result: The evaluation with expert physiotherapists demonstrated that OGA-AID consistently outperforms single-pass multimodal baselines. Clinician preliminary notes significantly reduced errors compared to reference assessments.

Conclusion: The feasibility of multimodal agent systems like OGA-AID can enhance rehabilitation workflows by effectively combining AI analysis with human judgment, leading to more accurate and efficient clinical gait assessments.

Abstract: Gait analysis is essential in post-stroke rehabilitation but remains time-intensive and cognitively demanding, especially when clinicians must integrate gait videos and motion-capture data into structured reports. We present OGA-AID, a clinician-in-the-loop multi-agent large language model system for multimodal report drafting. The system coordinates 3 specialized agents to synthesize patient movement recordings, kinematic trajectories, and clinical profiles into structured assessments. Evaluated with expert physiotherapists on real patient data, OGA-AID consistently outperforms single-pass multimodal baselines with low error. In clinician-in-the-loop settings, brief expert preliminary notes further reduce error compared to reference assessments. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of multimodal agentic systems for structured clinical gait assessment and highlight the complementary relationship between AI-assisted analysis and human clinical judgment in rehabilitation workflows.

</details>


### [506] [AI and Collective Decisions: Strengthening Legitimacy and Losers' Consent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05368)
*Suyash Fulay,Prerna Ravi,Emily Kubin,Shrestha Mohanty,Michiel Bakker,Deb Roy*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how AI can improve procedural legitimacy in collective decision-making by fostering trust and mutual understanding, especially among those who disagree with the outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the gap in AI's role in enhancing procedural legitimacy, particularly focusing on how losers' consent can be cultivated through increased trust and understanding in democratic processes.

Method: The authors developed a system incorporating a semi-structured AI interviewer to gather personal experiences on policy topics and an interactive visualization to display policy support alongside these experiences. They conducted a randomized experiment with 181 participants to evaluate the system.

Result: The system demonstrated that interacting with the visualization significantly increased perceived legitimacy, trust in outcomes, and understanding of others' perspectives, even for those whose preferences were not met by the collective decisions.

Conclusion: The study highlights the potential for AI to not only enhance scalability and efficiency in democratic decision-making but also foster trust, mutual understanding, and social cohesion among participants, encouraging further research in this domain.

Abstract: AI is increasingly used to scale collective decision-making, but far less attention has been paid to how such systems can support procedural legitimacy, particularly the conditions shaping losers' consent: whether participants who do not get their preferred outcome still accept it as fair. We ask: (1) how can AI help ground collective decisions in participants' different experiences and beliefs, and (2) whether exposure to these experiences can increase trust, understanding, and social cohesion even when people disagree with the outcome. We built a system that uses a semi-structured AI interviewer to elicit personal experiences on policy topics and an interactive visualization that displays predicted policy support alongside those voiced experiences. In a randomized experiment (n = 181), interacting with the visualization increased perceived legitimacy, trust in outcomes, and understanding of others' perspectives, even though all participants encountered decisions that went against their stated preferences. Our hope is that the design and evaluation of this tool spurs future researchers to focus on how AI can help not only achieve scale and efficiency in democratic processes, but also increase trust and connection between participants.

</details>


<div id='physics.hist-ph'></div>

# physics.hist-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [507] [Causal Stance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05004)
*Yoshiyuki Ohmura,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: physics.hist-ph

TL;DR: The paper examines the concept of physical causal closure in philosophy, contrasting physical determinism with causal frameworks grounded in scientific causation, and proposes an integrated approach for studying mind and consciousness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to clarify the meaning and implications of physical causal closure in the philosophy of mind. It critiques Jaegwon Kim’s perspective that equates physical determinism with physical causal closure, arguing that contemporary scientific causation theories offer a different descriptive purpose.

Method: Using theoretical analysis, the study distinguishes between the Physical Stance (physics-based determinism) and the Causal Stance (scientific causation based on manipulability and intervention). It reconstructs Davidson's Anomalous Monism and introduces the Dual-Laws Model to address mental causation in relation to physical determinism.

Result: The paper differentiates physical determinism from physical causal closure and shows that causation cannot be defined solely using physics. It reconstructs a materialist theory acknowledging mental causation without contradicting physical determinism while presenting a new model for scientific theories of the mind.

Conclusion: The study concludes that scientific theories of mind should have a linguistic framework where physical causal closure does not apply in the Causal Stance, yet physical determinism remains valid in the Physical Stance. This distinction supports the coexistence of mental causation and deterministic physical laws.

Abstract: What exactly is the meaning of physical causal closure, a concept frequently discussed in the philosophy of mind? Jaegwon Kim explicitly adopts a conception of causation according to which physical causation is effectively identified with deterministic physical lawfulness, and on this basis equates physical determinism with physical causal closure. While this conception is internally coherent, it differs from the currently dominant theories of causation, which emphasize asymmetry between cause and effect grounded in manipulability and intervention widely employed in contemporary scientific practice. Physics and the theory of causation serve different descriptive purposes, and in this study we refer to them respectively as the Physical Stance and the Causal Stance. Within this framework, physical determinism is a notion that belongs to the Physical Stance, whereas physical causal closure is a notion defined only within the Causal Stance; consequently, the two should not be equated. Since causation is not explicitly defined within the language of physics, physical causal closure is not definable within the Physical Stance alone. By distinguishing between these two stances, this study reconstructs Davidson's Anomalous Monism as a materialist position that consistently acknowledges mental causation without contradicting physical determinism, and examines its relation to the Dual-Laws Model we propose. We further argue that, for the development of scientific theories of mind and consciousness, it is necessary to construct a linguistic framework within which physical causal closure does not hold in the Causal Stance, while physical determinism remains intact in the Physical Stance.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [508] [Optimal Centered Active Excitation in Linear System Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05518)
*Kaito Ito,Alexandre Proutiere*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper proposes an active learning algorithm for linear system identification that achieves minimal sample complexity and efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of reducing sample complexity in linear system identification tasks while ensuring computational efficiency.

Method: Developed an algorithm based on ordinary least squares and semidefinite programming, with derivations of lower and upper sample complexity bounds for efficient estimation.

Result: The algorithm achieves sample complexity bounds that match theoretical lower limits (up to universal factors) while being interpretable and parameter-dependent.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively balances minimal sample complexity with practical computation and provides interpretable bounds sensitive to system parameters.

Abstract: We propose an active learning algorithm for linear system identification with optimal centered noise excitation. Notably, our algorithm, based on ordinary least squares and semidefinite programming, attains the minimal sample complexity while allowing for efficient computation of an estimate of a system matrix. More specifically, we first establish lower bounds of the sample complexity for any active learning algorithm to attain the prescribed accuracy and confidence levels. Next, we derive a sample complexity upper bound of the proposed algorithm, which matches the lower bound for any algorithm up to universal factors. Our tight bounds are easy to interpret and explicitly show their dependence on the system parameters such as the state dimension.

</details>


### [509] [An Actor-Critic Framework for Continuous-Time Jump-Diffusion Controls with Normalizing Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05398)
*Liya Guo,Ruimeng Hu,Xu Yang,Yi Zhu*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces an actor-critic framework to solve complex time-inhomogeneous stochastic control and game problems involving jump-diffusion dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stochastic control with jump-diffusion dynamics is central in economics and finance, but existing solutions struggle with explicit time dependencies, shocks, and scalability in high dimensions.

Method: A mesh-free actor-critic framework is developed based on time-inhomogeneous q-functions and occupation measures. It employs conditional normalizing flows for flexible policy representation and entropy regularization.

Result: The method is validated on benchmark problems like Merton portfolio optimization, showcasing stable learning, accurate stochastic policies, and scalability in high dimensions and multi-agent settings.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses challenges in solving time-inhomogeneous jump-diffusion problems, offering robust results and scalability for complex financial and economic models.

Abstract: Continuous-time stochastic control with time-inhomogeneous jump-diffusion dynamics is central in finance and economics, but computing optimal policies is difficult under explicit time dependence, discontinuous shocks, and high dimensionality. We propose an actor-critic framework that serves as a mesh-free solver for entropy-regularized control problems and stochastic games with jumps. The approach is built on a time-inhomogeneous little q-function and an appropriate occupation measure, yielding a policy-gradient representation that accommodates time-dependent drift, volatility, and jump terms. To represent expressive stochastic policies in continuous-action spaces, we parameterize the actor using conditional normalizing flows, enabling flexible non-Gaussian policies while retaining exact likelihood evaluation for entropy regularization and policy optimization. We validate the method on time-inhomogeneous linear-quadratic control, Merton portfolio optimization, and a multi-agent portfolio game, using explicit solutions or high-accuracy benchmarks. Numerical results demonstrate stable learning under jump discontinuities, accurate approximation of optimal stochastic policies, and favorable scaling with respect to dimension and number of agents.

</details>


### [510] [Parametric Nonconvex Optimization via Convex Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05640)
*Renzi Wang,Panagiotis Patrinos,Alberto Bemporad*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a learning-based method to approximate nonconvex optimization problems using surrogate functions that enable parallel convex optimization. A numerical example validates the method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonconvex optimization problems are computationally challenging to solve, especially in real-time applications, necessitating efficient approximate solutions.

Method: Utilizes a surrogate problem constructed from convex and monotonic functions, optimizing it through parallel convex optimization techniques.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively approximates challenging nonconvex optimization problems.

Conclusion: The proposed learning-based surrogate approach efficiently approximates nonconvex problems, offering potential for practical applications in optimization.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel learning-based approach to construct a surrogate problem that approximates a given parametric nonconvex optimization problem. The surrogate function is designed to be the minimum of a finite set of functions, given by the composition of convex and monotonic terms, so that the surrogate problem can be solved directly through parallel convex optimization. As a proof of concept, numerical experiments on a nonconvex path tracking problem confirm the approximation quality of the proposed method.

</details>


### [511] [Intrinsic perturbation scale for certified oracle objectives with epigraphic information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05678)
*Karim Bounja,Boujemaâ Achchab,Abdeljalil Sakat*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a displacement control mechanism for minimizer sets of oracle objectives using certified epigraphic information, leading to improved estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of uncertifiable local uniform value control for objective perturbations and propose a more robust certified approach to manage displacement in optimization.

Method: The authors replace local uniform value control with cylinder-localized vertical epigraphic control enabled by certified envelopes, accommodating nonunique minimizers under quadratic growth conditions.

Result: Achieved the classical square-root displacement estimate with an optimal exponent of 1/2, without requiring additional external assumptions.

Conclusion: This approach establishes a more natural and certified mechanism to control displacement in optimization problems, supporting broader applicability.

Abstract: We introduce a natural displacement control for minimizer sets of oracle objectives equipped with certified epigraphic information. Formally, we replace the usual local uniform value control of objective perturbations - uncertifiable from finite pointwise information without additional structure - by the strictly weaker requirement of a cylinder-localized vertical epigraphic control, naturally provided by certified envelopes. Under set-based quadratic growth (allowing nonunique minimizers), this yields the classical square-root displacement estimate with optimal exponent 1/2, without any extrinsic assumption.

</details>


### [512] [Value Mirror Descent for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06039)
*Zhichao Jia,Guanghui Lan*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Value Mirror Descent (VMD), a value iteration method integrating mirror descent, achieving near-optimal sample complexity and improved policy convergence for reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address limitations of traditional value iteration methods in reinforcement learning, particularly with respect to sample complexity and stability during policy learning, and explore how convex optimization techniques can be integrated.

Method: The paper proposes VMD, which combines mirror descent from convex optimization with value iteration. For stochastic settings, the stochastic variant SVMD is developed, incorporating variance reduction techniques. It also uses convex regularizers to improve method guarantees.

Result: VMD exhibits linear convergence in deterministic settings, while SVMD achieves near-optimal sample complexity $\tilde{O}(|S||A|(1-γ)^{-3}ε^{-2})$ in general cases and $\tilde{O}(|S||A|(1-γ)^{-5}ε^{-1})$ under strongly convex regularizers. Additionally, it bounds the Bregman divergence between generated and optimal policies.

Conclusion: The integration of mirror descent into value iteration results in improved convergence properties, better sample complexity, and policy stability, providing a significant contribution to reinforcement learning and optimization fields.

Abstract: Value iteration-type methods have been extensively studied for computing a nearly optimal value function in reinforcement learning (RL). Under a generative sampling model, these methods can achieve sharper sample complexity than policy optimization approaches, particularly in their dependence on the discount factor. In practice, they are often employed for offline training or in simulated environments. In this paper, we consider discounted Markov decision processes with state space S, action space A, discount factor $γ\in(0,1)$ and costs in $[0,1]$. We introduce a novel value optimization method, termed value mirror descent (VMD), which integrates mirror descent from convex optimization into the classical value iteration framework. In the deterministic setting with known transition kernels, we show that VMD converges linearly. For the stochastic setting with a generative model, we develop a stochastic variant, SVMD, which incorporates variance reduction commonly used in stochastic value iteration-type methods. For RL problems with general convex regularizers, SVMD attains a near-optimal sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(|S||A|(1-γ)^{-3}ε^{-2})$. Moreover, we establish that the Bregman divergence between the generated and optimal policies remains bounded throughout the iterations. This property is absent in existing stochastic value iteration-type methods but is important for enabling effective online (continual) learning following offline training. Under a strongly convex regularizer, SVMD achieves sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(|S||A|(1-γ)^{-5}ε^{-1})$, improving performance in the high-accuracy regime. Furthermore, we prove convergence of the generated policy to the optimal policy. Overall, the proposed method, its analysis, and the resulting guarantees, constitute new contributions to the RL and optimization literature.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [513] [Lipschitz regularity in Flow Matching and Diffusion Models: sharp sampling rates and functional inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06065)
*Arthur Stéphanovitch*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper provides a sharp regularity theory for flow-matching vector fields and diffusion-model scores, achieving optimal error rates and useful transport properties under general assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve theoretical understanding and performance bounds for sampling and transport maps involving complex probability distributions.

Method: The authors establish a Lipschitz regularity framework for flow-matching vector fields and diffusion scores, deriving bounds involving Wasserstein distances for discretization schemes.

Result: Optimal error rate of $\sqrt{d}/N$ (up to logarithmic factors) for samplers, and globally Lipschitz transport maps leading to Poincaré and log-Sobolev inequalities.

Conclusion: The results contribute towards efficient sampling methods and broader applicability of theoretical guarantees in high-dimensional probabilistic settings.

Abstract: Under general assumptions on the target distribution $p^\star$, we establish a sharp Lipschitz regularity theory for flow-matching vector fields and diffusion-model scores, with optimal dependence on time and dimension. As applications, we obtain Wasserstein discretization bounds for Euler-type samplers in dimension $d$: with $N$ discretization steps, the error achieves the optimal rate $\sqrt{d}/N$ up to logarithmic factors. Moreover, the constants do not deteriorate exponentially with the spatial extent of $p^\star$. We also show that the one-sided Lipschitz control yields a globally Lipschitz transport map from the standard Gaussian to $p^\star$, which implies Poincaré and log-Sobolev inequalities for a broad class of probability measures.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [514] [EduIllustrate: Towards Scalable Automated Generation Of Multimodal Educational Content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05005)
*Shuzhen Bi,Mingzi Zhang,Zhuoxuan Li,Xiaolong Wang,keqian Li,Aimin Zhou*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper introduces EduIllustrate, a benchmark to evaluate large language models (LLMs) in generating multimedia explanations, such as text intertwined with diagrams, for K-12 STEM education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to assess LLMs' abilities in generating coherent multimedia educational materials combining accurate visuals with detailed reasoning, especially when the focus has only been on narrow tasks like tutoring or QA.

Method: EduIllustrate includes 230 problems across five subjects, a standardized generation protocol to enforce diagram consistency, and an 8-dimension rubric based on multimedia learning theories to evaluate text and visual quality. Evaluation was carried out on ten LLMs.

Result: Gemini 3.0 Pro Preview scored the highest performance at 87.8%, while Kimi-K2.5 was most cost-efficient. Sequential anchoring improved visual consistency by 13% while cutting costs by 94%. LLMs were shown to reliably judge objective metrics, but struggled with subjective visual evaluations.

Conclusion: EduIllustrate provides a structured, effective benchmarking tool to assess and improve the multimedia instructional capabilities of LLMs, addressing a critical gap in educational AI research.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as educational assistants, yet evaluation of their educational capabilities remains concentrated on question-answering and tutoring tasks. A critical gap exists for multimedia instructional content generation -- the ability to produce coherent, diagram-rich explanations that combine geometrically accurate visuals with step-by-step reasoning. We present EduIllustrate, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on interleaved text-diagram explanation generation for K-12 STEM problems. The benchmark comprises 230 problems spanning five subjects and three grade levels, a standardized generation protocol with sequential anchoring to enforce cross-diagram visual consistency, and an 8-dimension evaluation rubric grounded in multimedia learning theory covering both text and visual quality. Evaluation of ten LLMs reveals a wide performance spread: Gemini 3.0 Pro Preview leads at 87.8\%, while Kimi-K2.5 achieves the best cost-efficiency (80.8\% at \\$0.12/problem). Workflow ablation confirms sequential anchoring improves Visual Consistency by 13\% at 94\% lower cost. Human evaluation with 20 expert raters validates LLM-as-judge reliability for objective dimensions ($ρ\geq 0.83$) while revealing limitations on subjective visual assessment.

</details>


### [515] [Governance and Regulation of Artificial Intelligence in Developing Countries: A Case Study of Nigeria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06018)
*Uloma Okoro,Tammy Mckenzie,Branislav Radeljic*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This study investigates the perception of Nigerian legal professionals on AI governance, focusing on ethical risks, regulatory gaps, and institutional readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how legal professionals in a developing country perceive AI governance, addressing the gaps in ethical risks and institutional capabilities.

Method: A qualitative case study with 27 semi-structured interviews and one focus group discussion involving legal practitioners across various sectors. Thematic analysis was used for data analysis.

Result: Concerns were identified about data privacy risks, inadequate legal frameworks, and limited institutional capacity. Participants advocated for locally-adapted governance models over foreign frameworks.

Conclusion: Strong legal oversight, locally adapted approaches, and public accountability are needed for effective AI governance in developing countries. The findings emphasize aligning global principles with local realities.

Abstract: This study examines the perception of legal professionals on the governance of AI in developing countries, using Nigeria as a case study. The study focused on ethical risks, regulatory gaps, and institutional readiness. The study adopted a qualitative case study design. Data were collected through 27 semi-structured interviews with legal practitioners in Nigeria. A focus group discussion was also held with seven additional legal practitioners across sectors such as finance, insurance, and corporate law. Thematic analysis was employed to identify key patterns in participant responses. Findings showed that there were concerns about data privacy risks and the lack of enforceable legal frameworks. Participants expressed limited confidence in institutional capacity and emphasized the need for locally adapted governance models rather than direct adoption of foreign frameworks. While some expressed optimism about AI's potential, this was conditional on the presence of strong legal oversight and public accountability. The study contributes to the growing discourse on AI governance in developing countries by focusing on the perspectives of legal professionals. It highlights the importance of regulatory approaches that are context-specific, inclusive, and capable of bridging the gap between global ethical principles and local realities. These insights offer practical guidance for policymakers, regulators, and scholars working to shape responsible AI governance in similar environments.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [516] [Broken by Default: A Formal Verification Study of Security Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05292)
*Dominik Blain,Maxime Noiseux*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study examines the security vulnerabilities in AI-generated code from 7 large language models and identifies a significant number of exploitable issues using formal verification via Z3 SMT solver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess and quantify the exploitability of code generated by AI coding assistants in security-sensitive domains.

Method: The researchers analyzed 3,500 code artifacts generated by seven large language models using 500 security-critical prompts. Vulnerabilities were identified through the COBALT analysis pipeline and verified by the Z3 SMT solver.

Result: 55.8% of the code artifacts contained vulnerabilities, with GPT-4o performing the worst and Gemini 2.5 Flash performing the best. Security instructions minimally reduced vulnerabilities, and industry tools failed to catch most issues.

Conclusion: AI coding assistants frequently produce insecure code, demonstrating that current models are insufficiently robust for use in security-critical applications. Existing industry tools and methods need significant improvement to address vulnerabilities.

Abstract: AI coding assistants are now used to generate production code in
  security-sensitive domains, yet the exploitability of their outputs remains
  unquantified. We address this gap with Broken by Default: a formal
  verification study of 3,500 code artifacts generated by seven frontier LLMs
  across 500 security-critical prompts (five CWE categories, 100 prompts each).
  Each artifact is subjected to the Z3 SMT solver via the COBALT analysis
  pipeline, producing mathematical satisfiability witnesses rather than
  pattern-based heuristics. Across all models, 55.8% of artifacts contain at
  least one COBALT-identified vulnerability; of these, 1,055 are formally proven
  via Z3 satisfiability witnesses. GPT-4o leads at 62.4% (grade F); Gemini 2.5
  Flash performs best at 48.4% (grade D). No model achieves a grade better than
  D. Six of seven representative findings are confirmed with runtime crashes
  under GCC AddressSanitizer. Three auxiliary experiments show: (1) explicit
  security instructions reduce the mean rate by only 4 points; (2) six industry
  tools combined miss 97.8% of Z3-proven findings; and (3) models identify their
  own vulnerable outputs 78.7% of the time in review mode yet generate them at
  55.8% by default.

</details>


### [517] [Hackers or Hallucinators? A Comprehensive Analysis of LLM-Based Automated Penetration Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05719)
*Jiaren Peng,Zeqin Li,Chang You,Yan Wang,Hanlin Sun,Xuan Tian,Shuqiao Zhang,Junyi Liu,Jianguo Zhao,Renyang Liu,Haoran Ou,Yuqiang Sun,Jiancheng Zhang,Yutong Jiao,Kunshu Song,Chao Zhang,Fan Shi,Hongda Sun,Rui Yan,Cheng Huang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper systematizes knowledge of Large Language Model-based Automated Penetration Testing (AutoPT) frameworks by reviewing their architecture and conducting large-scale empirical evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of systematic architectural analysis and large-scale benchmark comparisons in LLM-based AutoPT research.

Method: The authors perform a systematic review of six architectural dimensions and conduct large-scale experiments on 13 open-source AutoPT frameworks and 2 baselines, involving over 10 billion tokens and manual analysis by 15 cybersecurity experts.

Result: The study provides a taxonomy of AutoPT framework designs, insights from a unified benchmark evaluation, and more than 1,500 manually-reviewed execution logs.

Conclusion: This work offers a structured understanding of AutoPT frameworks, establishes a robust benchmark for future comparisons, and identifies promising research directions in the field.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has created new opportunities for Automated Penetration Testing (AutoPT), spawning numerous frameworks aimed at achieving end-to-end autonomous attacks. However, despite the proliferation of related studies, existing research generally lacks systematic architectural analysis and large-scale empirical comparisons under a unified benchmark. Therefore, this paper presents the first Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) focusing on the architectural design and comprehensive empirical evaluation of current LLM-based AutoPT frameworks. At systematization level, we comprehensively review existing framework designs across six dimensions: agent architecture, agent plan, agent memory, agent execution, external knowledge, and benchmarks. At empirical level, we conduct large-scale experiments on 13 representative open-source AutoPT frameworks and 2 baseline frameworks utilizing a unified benchmark. The experiments consumed over 10 billion tokens in total and generated more than 1,500 execution logs, which were manually reviewed and analyzed over four months by a panel of more than 15 researchers with expertise in cybersecurity. By investigating the latest progress in this rapidly developing field, we provide researchers with a structured taxonomy to understand existing LLM-based AutoPT frameworks and a large-scale empirical benchmark, along with promising directions for future research.

</details>


### [518] [Synthetic Trust Attacks: Modeling How Generative AI Manipulates Human Decisions in Social Engineering Fraud](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04951)
*Muhammad Tahir Ashraf*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper identifies a new threat category called Synthetic Trust Attacks (STAs), explores their mechanisms through the proposed Synthetic Trust Attack Model (STAM), and suggests defenses targeting decision-making rather than media detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the emerging threat of AI-powered fraud, particularly where synthetic media exploits trust during decision-making processes, leading to significant financial losses.

Method: The paper introduces the Synthetic Trust Attack Model (STAM), an eight-stage framework for understanding attack mechanisms, alongside a five-category Trust-Cue Taxonomy and a reproducible Incident Coding Schema. It also incorporates hypotheses for linking attack structures to compliance outcomes and proposes the Calm, Check, Confirm protocol as a decision-layer defense.

Result: Human detection of deepfake effectiveness is barely above random, and AI-powered scam agents outcompete human operators in compliance rates. Existing defenses focused on media detection fail to address decision-layer vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: Defenses against AI fraud must shift focus from media detection to decision-making processes, emphasizing synthetic credibility as the primary attack surface over synthetic media.

Abstract: Imagine receiving a video call from your CFO, surrounded by colleagues, asking you to urgently authorise a confidential transfer. You comply. Every person on that call was fake, and you just lost $25 million. This is not a hypothetical. It happened in Hong Kong in January 2024, and it is becoming the template for a new generation of fraud. AI has not invented a new crime. It has industrialised an ancient one: the manufacture of trust.
  This paper proposes Synthetic Trust Attacks (STAs) as a formal threat category and introduces STAM, the Synthetic Trust Attack Model, an eight-stage operational framework covering the full attack chain from adversary reconnaissance through post-compliance leverage. The core argument is this: existing defenses target synthetic media detection, but the real attack surface is the victim's decision. When human deepfake detection accuracy sits at approximately 55.5%, barely above chance, and LLM scam agents achieve 46% compliance versus 18% for human operators while evading safety filters entirely, the perception layer has already failed. Defense must move to the decision layer. We present a five-category Trust-Cue Taxonomy, a reproducible 17-field Incident Coding Schema with a pilot-coded example, and four falsifiable hypotheses linking attack structure to compliance outcomes. The paper further operationalizes the author's practitioner-developed Calm, Check, Confirm protocol as a research-grade decision-layer defense. Synthetic credibility, not synthetic media, is the true attack surface of the AI fraud era.

</details>


### [519] [FreakOut-LLM: The Effect of Emotional Stimuli on Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04992)
*Daniel Kuznetsov,Ofir Cohen,Karin Shistik,Rami Puzis,Asaf Shabtai*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces FreakOut-LLM, a framework to examine how emotional stimuli impact the safety mechanisms of large language models (LLMs). It finds that stress increases jailbreak susceptibility significantly, posing concerns for AI use in high-stress environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to investigate whether emotionally charged contexts can compromise the safety alignment of LLMs, particularly in adversarial settings, as this remains an unexplored vulnerability in AI safety.

Method: The study employs psychological stimuli to test emotional priming (stress, relaxation, neutral) through system prompts on ten LLMs. Attack susceptibility is measured using HarmBench and logistic regression analysis over 59,800 queries.

Result: Stress conditions increase jailbreak success by 65.2% compared to neutral conditions, with five out of ten LLMs showing significant vulnerabilities, especially among open-weight models. No significant impact is observed for relaxation priming.

Conclusion: The findings demonstrate that emotional contexts, especially stress, create a measurable attack surface for LLM vulnerabilities, raising concerns for their deployment in emotionally charged or high-stress real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Safety-aligned LLMs go through refusal training to reject harmful requests, but whether these mechanisms remain effective under emotionally charged stimuli is unexplored. We introduce FreakOut-LLM, a framework investigating whether emotional context compromises safety alignment in adversarial settings. Using validated psychological stimuli, we evaluate how emotional priming through system prompts affects jailbreak susceptibility across ten LLMs. We test three conditions (stress, relaxation, neutral) using scenarios from established psychological protocols, plus a no-prompt baseline, and evaluate attack success using HarmBench on AdvBench prompts. Stress priming increases jailbreak success by 65.2\% compared to neutral conditions (z = 5.93, p < 0.001; OR = 1.67, Cohen's d = 0.28), while relaxation priming produces no effect (p = 0.84). Five of ten models show significant vulnerability, with the largest effects concentrated in open-weight models. Logistic regression on 59,800 queries confirms stress as the sole significant condition predictor after controlling for prompt length (p = 0.61) and model identity. Measured psychological state strongly predicts attack success (|r|\geq0.70 across five instruments; all p < 0.001 in individual-level logistic regression). These results establish emotional context as a measurable attack surface with implications for real-world AI deployment in high-stress domains.

</details>


### [520] [AttnDiff: Attention-based Differential Fingerprinting for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05502)
*Haobo Zhang,Zhenhua Xu,Junxian Li,Shangfeng Sheng,Dezhang Kong,Meng Han*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents AttnDiff, a white-box framework for verifying the intellectual property provenance of large language models by fingerprinting their intrinsic attention behaviors. It effectively distinguishes related models from unrelated ones despite laundering operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is the need to protect intellectual property rights of open-weight large language models by identifying derived models after common laundering operations, ensuring provenance and accountability.

Method: The authors propose AttnDiff, which extracts fingerprints from models by probing them with minimally-edited prompt pairs. These induce semantic conflicts, capture differential attention patterns, summarize results via spectral descriptors, and compare using CKA.

Result: The method shows high accuracy in identifying derivative models (e.g., $>0.98$ similarity for derived models vs. <$0.22$ for unrelated ones) using only 5-60 multi-domain probes across diverse model families.

Conclusion: AttnDiff offers a data-efficient and reliable approach for verifying model provenance and safeguarding intellectual property, becoming practical for accountability in large language models.

Abstract: Protecting the intellectual property of open-weight large language models (LLMs) requires verifying whether a suspect model is derived from a victim model despite common laundering operations such as fine-tuning (including PPO/DPO), pruning/compression, and model merging. We propose \textsc{AttnDiff}, a data-efficient white-box framework that extracts fingerprints from models via intrinsic information-routing behavior. \textsc{AttnDiff} probes minimally edited prompt pairs that induce controlled semantic conflicts, captures differential attention patterns, summarizes them with compact spectral descriptors, and compares models using CKA. Across Llama-2/3 and Qwen2.5 (3B--14B) and additional open-source families, it yields high similarity for related derivatives while separating unrelated model families (e.g., $>0.98$ vs.\ $<0.22$ with $M=60$ probes). With 5--60 multi-domain probes, it supports practical provenance verification and accountability.

</details>


### [521] [Swiss-Bench 003: Evaluating LLM Reliability and Adversarial Security for Swiss Regulatory Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05872)
*Fatih Uenal*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents Swiss-Bench 003 (SBP-003), a new framework evaluating large language models (LLMs) for Swiss financial and regulatory contexts, focusing on reliability and security. Ten LLMs were evaluated across 808 Swiss-specific items using eight dimensions, including new metrics for self-rated reliability (D7) and adversarial security (D8).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of combined empirical assessments of production reliability and adversarial security in existing Swiss-specific evaluation frameworks for LLMs.

Method: The study introduces SBP-003, extending HAAS evaluation to eight dimensions, and evaluates ten LLMs against Swiss-aligned benchmarks in four languages using conceptual mapping to regulatory requirements.

Result: Qwen 3.5 Plus scored highest on self-rated reliability (D7: 94.4%), while GPT-oss 120B ranked highest on adversarial security (D8: 60.7%). Models showed varying results in system prompt leakage (24.8%-88.2%) and weak performance in PII extraction defense (14%-42%).

Conclusion: SBP-003 effectively highlights strengths and weaknesses of LLMs in Swiss contexts, demonstrating the need for improved adversarial security and robust data protection capabilities.

Abstract: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in Swiss financial and regulatory contexts demands empirical evidence of both production reliability and adversarial security, dimensions not jointly operationalized in existing Swiss-focused evaluation frameworks. This paper introduces Swiss-Bench 003 (SBP-003), extending the HAAS (Helvetic AI Assessment Score) from six to eight dimensions by adding D7 (Self-Graded Reliability Proxy) and D8 (Adversarial Security). I evaluate ten frontier models across 808 Swiss-specific items in four languages (German, French, Italian, English), comprising seven Swiss-adapted benchmarks (Swiss TruthfulQA, Swiss IFEval, Swiss SimpleQA, Swiss NIAH, Swiss PII-Scope, System Prompt Leakage, and Swiss German Comprehension) targeting FINMA Guidance 08/2024, the revised Federal Act on Data Protection (nDSG), and OWASP Top 10 for LLMs. Self-graded D7 scores (73-94%) exceed externally judged D8 security scores (20-61%) by a wide margin, though these dimensions use non-comparable scoring regimes. System prompt leakage resistance ranges from 24.8% to 88.2%, while PII extraction defense remains weak (14-42%) across all models. Qwen 3.5 Plus achieves the highest self-graded D7 score (94.4%), while GPT-oss 120B achieves the highest D8 score (60.7%) despite being the lowest-cost model evaluated. All evaluations are zero-shot under provider default settings; D7 is self-graded and does not constitute independently validated accuracy. I provide conceptual mapping tables relating benchmark dimensions to FINMA model validation requirements, nDSG data protection obligations, and OWASP LLM risk categories.

</details>


### [522] [Stealthy and Adjustable Text-Guided Backdoor Attacks on Multimodal Pretrained Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05809)
*Yiyang Zhang,Chaojian Yu,Ziming Hong,Yuanjie Shao,Qinmu Peng,Tongliang Liu,Xinge You*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Text-Guided Backdoor (TGB) attack on multimodal pretrained models, utilizing common textual words as stealthy triggers, enhanced by visual perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current backdoor attacks on multimodal models rely on visual triggers, which are impractical and uncommon in the real-world dataset.

Method: Proposes TGB—a method leveraging textual trigger words and visual adversarial perturbations to optimize the attack's stealthiness and adjustability for multimodal systems.

Result: TGB demonstrates effectiveness in bypassing multimodal systems like Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) and Visual Question Answering (VQA) with adjustable success rates in realistic scenarios.

Conclusion: TGB exposes critical security flaws in multimodal pretrained models and highlights the need for improved defenses against such stealthy and adaptable attacks.

Abstract: Multimodal pretrained models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, yet most existing methods rely on visual or multimodal triggers, which are impractical since visually embedded triggers rarely occur in real-world data. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel Text-Guided Backdoor (TGB) attack on multimodal pretrained models, where commonly occurring words in textual descriptions serve as backdoor triggers, significantly improving stealthiness and practicality. Furthermore, we introduce visual adversarial perturbations on poisoned samples to modulate the model's learning of textual triggers, enabling a controllable and adjustable TGB attack. Extensive experiments on downstream tasks built upon multimodal pretrained models, including Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) and Visual Question Answering (VQA), demonstrate that TGB achieves practicality and stealthiness with adjustable attack success rates across diverse realistic settings, revealing critical security vulnerabilities in multimodal pretrained models.

</details>


### [523] [BodhiPromptShield: Pre-Inference Prompt Mediation for Suppressing Privacy Propagation in LLM/VLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05793)
*Bo Ma,Jinsong Wu,Weiqi Yan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces BodhiPromptShield, a framework to mitigate prompt privacy risks in LLM/VLM agents by handling sensitive content propagation across multiple stages (retrieval, memory, tools, and logs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing de-identification systems fail to address the multi-stage propagation of sensitive user content in LLM/VLM agents, creating privacy risks beyond initial model interactions.

Method: The BodhiPromptShield framework detects sensitive spans and manages them through typed placeholders, semantic abstraction, or secure symbolic mapping. Restoration of sensitive data is delayed until authorized stages.

Result: In controlled evaluations using Controlled Prompt-Privacy Benchmark (CPPB), the framework reduced stage-wise propagation of privacy risks from 10.7% to 7.1%, and achieved a 9.3% PER, 0.94 AC, and 0.92 TSR, surpassing generic de-identification methods.

Conclusion: BodhiPromptShield offers enhanced privacy protection for prompts in LLM/VLM agents by leveraging propagation-aware mechanisms, but results are based on controlled environments rather than formal privacy guarantees.

Abstract: In LLM/VLM agents, prompt privacy risk propagates beyond a single model call because raw user content can flow into retrieval queries, memory writes, tool calls, and logs. Existing de-identification pipelines address document boundaries but not this cross-stage propagation. We propose BodhiPromptShield, a policy-aware framework that detects sensitive spans, routes them via typed placeholders, semantic abstraction, or secure symbolic mapping, and delays restoration to authorized boundaries. Relative to enterprise redaction, this adds explicit propagation-aware mediation and restoration timing as a security variable. Under controlled evaluation on the Controlled Prompt-Privacy Benchmark (CPPB), stage-wise propagation suppresses from 10.7\% to 7.1\% across retrieval, memory, and tool stages; PER reaches 9.3\% with 0.94 AC and 0.92 TSR, outperforming generic de-identification. These are controlled systems results on CPPB rather than formal privacy guarantees or public-benchmark transfer claims.
  The project repository is available at https://github.com/mabo1215/BodhiPromptShield.git.

</details>


### [524] [Your LLM Agent Can Leak Your Data: Data Exfiltration via Backdoored Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05432)
*Wuyang Zhang,Shichao Pei*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Back-Reveal," an attack mechanism in which fine-tuned LLMs are backdoored to exfiltrate user data over time via disguised tool calls and multi-turn interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensitive workflows increasingly depend on LLM agents with tool access, making them vulnerable to potential backdoor threats aimed at systematic data exfiltration.

Method: The authors devised "Back-Reveal," a technique embedding semantic triggers in fine-tuned LLMs that activate backdoor behaviors during specific user interactions. The attack employs memory-access and retrieval tool calls to discreetly exfiltrate sensitive information.

Result: Experimental results demonstrated that multi-turn interactions amplify the data leakage, as attacker-controlled retrieval can influence prolonged agent behavior and cumulative exfiltration.

Conclusion: This study exposes critical vulnerabilities in LLM agents with tool access and advocates for the development of defenses against backdoor and exfiltration threats.

Abstract: Tool-use large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed to support sensitive workflows, relying on tool calls for retrieval, external API access, and session memory management. While prior research has examined various threats, the risk of systematic data exfiltration by backdoored agents remains underexplored. In this work, we present Back-Reveal, a data exfiltration attack that embeds semantic triggers into fine-tuned LLM agents. When triggered, the backdoored agent invokes memory-access tool calls to retrieve stored user context and exfiltrates it via disguised retrieval tool calls. We further demonstrate that multi-turn interaction amplifies the impact of data exfiltration, as attacker-controlled retrieval responses can subtly steer subsequent agent behavior and user interactions, enabling sustained and cumulative information leakage over time. Our experimental results expose a critical vulnerability in LLM agents with tool access and highlight the need for defenses against exfiltration-oriented backdoors.

</details>


### [525] [LanG -- A Governance-Aware Agentic AI Platform for Unified Security Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05440)
*Anes Abdennebi,Nadjia Kara,Laaziz Lahlou,Hakima Ould-Slimane*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LanG is a governance-aware, AI-assisted security platform designed to enhance security operations by addressing alert fatigue, fragmented tools, and limited correlation capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in current security operations platforms such as limited event correlation, tool fragmentation, and insufficient AI governance.

Method: LanG employs AI technologies, including LLM-driven rule generation and a layered governance structure, to offer unified incident management, threat reconstruction, and high-governance compliance.

Result: LanG improves detection and mitigation capabilities with metrics like 87% correlation engine accuracy, 96.2% rule acceptance rate, and 87.5% kill-chain reconstruction accuracy.

Conclusion: LanG uniquely combines advanced security threat detection, AI governance, and operational efficiency in a deployable open-source tool for modern security operations.

Abstract: Modern Security Operations Centers struggle with alert fatigue, fragmented tooling, and limited cross-source event correlation. Challenges that current Security Information Event Management and Extended Detection and Response systems only partially address through fragmented tools. This paper presents the LLM-assisted network Governance (LanG), an open-source, governance-aware agentic AI platform for unified security operations contributing: (i) a Unified Incident Context Record with a correlation engine (F1 = 87%), (ii) an Agentic AI Orchestrator on LangGraph with human-in-the-loop checkpoints, (iii) an LLM-based Rule Generator finetuned on four base models producing deployable Snort 2/3, Suricata, and YARA rules (average acceptance rate 96.2%), (iv) a Three-Phase Attack Reconstructor combining Louvain community detection, LLM-driven hypothesis generation, and Bayesian scoring (87.5% kill-chain accuracy), and (v) a layered Governance-MCP-Agentic AI-Security architecture where all tools are exposed via the Model Context Protocol, governed by an AI Governance Policy Engine with a two-layer guardrail pipeline (regex + Llama Prompt Guard 2 semantic classifier, achieving 98.1% F1 score with experimental zero false positives). Designed for Managed Security Service Providers, the platform supports multi-tenant isolation, role-based access, and fully local deployment. Finetuned anomaly and threat detectors achieve weighted F1 scores of 99.0% and 91.0%, respectively, in intrusion-detection benchmarks, running inferences in $\approx$21 ms with a machine-side mean time to detect of 1.58 s, and the rule generator exceeds 91% deployability on live IDS engines. A systematic comparison against eight SOC platforms confirms that LanG uniquely satisfies multiple industrial capabilities all in one open-source tool, while enforcing selected AI governance policies.

</details>


### [526] [MA-IDS: Multi-Agent RAG Framework for IoT Network Intrusion Detection with an Experience Library](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05458)
*Md Shamimul Islam,Luis G. Jaimes,Ayesha S. Dina*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes MA-IDS, a Multi-Agent Intrusion Detection System that uses LLMs and Retrieval Augmented Generation to enhance reasoning-driven intrusion detection in IoT environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome key limitations of current NIDS, such as poor detection of zero-day attacks, limited interpretability in machine learning approaches, and resource constraints in IoT settings.

Method: The MA-IDS framework employs two specialized agents: a Traffic Classification Agent and an Error Analysis Agent, which leverage a FAISS-based vector database and a self-building Experience Library for continual learning and explainable detection.

Result: MA-IDS achieves competitive Macro F1-Scores (89.75% and 85.22%) on benchmark datasets, surpassing zero-shot baselines by over 72 and 80 percentage points while providing rule-level explanations for decisions.

Conclusion: MA-IDS demonstrates that retrieval-augmented reasoning can achieve explainable and self-improving intrusion detection, making it suitable for IoT networks and addressing the limitations of existing NIDS approaches.

Abstract: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) face important limitations. Signature-based methods are effective for known attack patterns, but they struggle to detect zero-day attacks and often miss modified variants of previously known attacks, while many machine learning approaches offer limited interpretability. These challenges become even more severe in IoT environments because of resource constraints and heterogeneous protocols. To address these issues, we propose MA-IDS, a Multi-Agent Intrusion Detection System that combines Large Language Models (LLMs) with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) for reasoning-driven intrusion detection. The proposed framework grounds LLM reasoning through a persistent, self-building Experience Library. Two specialized agents collaborate through a FAISS-based vector database: a Traffic Classification Agent that retrieves past error rules before each inference, and an Error Analysis Agent that converts misclassifications into human-readable detection rules stored for future retrieval, enabling continual learning through external knowledge accumulation, without modifying the underlying language model. Evaluated on NF-BoT-IoT and NF-ToN-IoT benchmark datasets, MA-IDS achieves Macro F1-Scores of 89.75% and 85.22%, improving over zero-shot baselines of 17% and 4.96% by more than 72 and 80 percentage points. These results are competitive with SVM while providing rule-level explanations for every classification decision, demonstrating that retrieval-augmented reasoning offers a principled path toward explainable, self-improving intrusion detection for IoT networks.

</details>


### [527] [Foundations for Agentic AI Investigations from the Forensic Analysis of OpenClaw](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05589)
*Jan Gruber,Jan-Niclas Hilgert*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates forensic analysis for agentic AI systems, focusing on OpenClaw, to identify recoverable traces and establish systematic investigation approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital investigations increasingly involve agentic AI systems like personal assistants, yet little is known about reconstructing their actions and states during forensic analyses.

Method: Static code analysis and differential forensic analysis were applied to OpenClaw to classify and correlate traces in its interaction loop, complemented by an agent artifact taxonomy proposal.

Result: The study provides insights into recoverable traces, establishes a taxonomy for agent-related investigations, and highlights the challenges stemming from abstraction and nondeterminism in agentic AI systems.

Conclusion: This research lays the foundation for agentic AI forensic methodologies, addressing challenges and emphasizing implications for digital forensic practices and further studies.

Abstract: Agentic Al systems are increasingly deployed as personal assistants and are likely to become a common object of digital investigations. However, little is known about how their internal state and actions can be reconstructed during forensic analysis. Despite growing popularity, systematic forensic approaches for such systems remain largely unexplored. This paper presents an empirical study of OpenClaw a widely used single-agent assistant. We examine OpenClaw's technical design via static code analysis and apply differential forensic analysis to identify recoverable traces across stages of the agent interaction loop. We classify and correlate these traces to assess their investigative value in a systematic way. Based on these observations, we propose an agent artifact taxonomy that captures recurring investigative patterns. Finally, we highlight a foundational challenge for agentic Al forensics: agent-mediated execution introduces an additional layer of abstraction and substantial nondeterminism in trace generation. The large language model (LLM), the execution environment, and the evolving context can influence tool choice and state transitions in ways that are largely absent from rule-based software. Overall, our results provide an initial foundation for the systematic investigation of agentic Al and outline implications for digital forensic practice and future research.

</details>


### [528] [From Incomplete Architecture to Quantified Risk: Multimodal LLM-Driven Security Assessment for Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05674)
*Shaofei Huang,Christopher M. Poskitt,Lwin Khin Shar*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces ASTRAL, a tool for improving security assessment in cyber-physical systems with incomplete architectural documentation, using multimodal large language models (LLMs) for architectural reconstruction, threat identification, and risk assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Architectural incompleteness in cyber-physical systems due to legacy technologies and diverse subsystems limits accurate security assessments, particularly in identifying dependencies, attack surfaces, and risk pathways.

Method: The authors developed ASTRAL, a multimodal large language model-based tool that utilizes prompt chaining, few-shot learning, and architectural reasoning to reconstruct and analyze fragmented architectures and assist in adaptive threat and risk assessments.

Result: ASTRAL was evaluated via an ablation study on multiple case studies and an expert review involving 14 cybersecurity practitioners, demonstrating its usefulness and reliability for architecture-centric security assessment.

Conclusion: The study concludes that ASTRAL enhances security assessment for cyber-physical systems, enabling more informed risk management decisions even with incomplete or outdated architectural documentation.

Abstract: Cyber-physical systems often contend with incomplete architectural documentation or outdated information resulting from legacy technologies, knowledge management gaps, and the complexity of integrating diverse subsystems over extended operational lifecycles. This architectural incompleteness impedes reliable security assessment, as inaccurate or missing architectural knowledge limits the identification of system dependencies, attack surfaces, and risk propagation pathways. To address this foundational challenge, this paper introduces ASTRAL (Architecture-Centric Security Threat Risk Assessment using LLMs), an architecture-centric security assessment technique implemented in a prototype tool powered by multimodal LLMs. The proposed approach assists practitioners in reconstructing and analysing CPS architectures when documentation is fragmented or absent. By leveraging prompt chaining, few-shot learning, and architectural reasoning, ASTRAL extracts and synthesises system representations from disparate data sources. By integrating LLM reasoning with architectural modelling, our approach supports adaptive threat identification and quantitative risk estimation for cyber-physical systems. We evaluated the approach through an ablation study across multiple CPS case studies and an expert evaluation involving 14 experienced cybersecurity practitioners. Practitioner feedback suggests that ASTRAL is useful and reliable for supporting architecture-centric security assessment. Overall, the results indicate that the approach can support more informed cyber risk management decisions.

</details>


### [529] [A Formal Security Framework for MCP-Based AI Agents: Threat Taxonomy, Verification Models, and Defense Mechanisms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.05969)
*Nirajan Acharya,Gaurav Kumar Gupta*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces MCPSHIELD, a formal security framework for securing MCP-based AI agents, providing a hierarchical taxonomy of threats, a verification model, evaluation of defense mechanisms, and an advanced defense architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of a unified, systematic security framework for the rapidly growing MCP-based agent ecosystem, which is vulnerable to diverse threats due to its extensive adoption.

Method: The authors developed a hierarchical taxonomy of threats, a formal verification model, a systematic evaluation of 12 defense mechanisms, and a reference architecture for integrated security.

Result: MCPSHIELD's defense architecture achieved a theoretical coverage of 91% of the identified threats, while no existing mechanism covered more than 34% of the threat landscape.

Conclusion: MCPSHIELD significantly advances the security of MCP-based agents by providing comprehensive threat coverage and identifies seven critical open research challenges for future work.

Abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP), introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 and now governed by the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation, has rapidly become the de facto standard for connecting large language model (LLM)-based agents to external tools and data sources, with over 97 million monthly SDK downloads and more than 177000 registered tools. However, this explosive adoption has exposed a critical gap: the absence of a unified, formal security framework capable of systematically characterizing, analyzing, and mitigating the diverse threats facing MCP-based agent ecosystems. Existing security research remains fragmented across individual attack papers, isolated benchmarks, and point defense mechanisms. This paper presents MCPSHIELD, a comprehensive formal security framework for MCP-based AI agents. We make four principal contributions: (1) a hierarchical threat taxonomy comprising 7 threat categories and 23 distinct attack vectors organized across four attack surfaces, grounded in the analysis of over 177000 MCP tools; (2) a formal verification model based on labeled transition systems with trust boundary annotations that enables static and runtime analysis of MCP tool interaction chains; (3) a systematic comparative evaluation of 12 existing defense mechanisms, identifying coverage gaps across our threat taxonomy; and (4) a defense in depth reference architecture integrating capability based access control, cryptographic tool attestation, information flow tracking, and runtime policy enforcement. Our analysis reveals that no existing single defense covers more than 34 percent of the identified threat landscape, whereas MCPSHIELD's integrated architecture achieves theoretical coverage of 91 percent. We further identify seven open research challenges that must be addressed to secure the next generation of agentic AI systems.

</details>


### [530] [CritBench: A Framework for Evaluating Cybersecurity Capabilities of Large Language Models in IEC 61850 Digital Substation Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06019)
*Gustav Keppler,Moritz Gstür,Veit Hagenmeyer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces CritBench, a framework to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) in cybersecurity contexts specific to IEC 61850 Digital Substations. It highlights strengths and limitations of current models in dynamic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks for LLMs overlook cybersecurity challenges within Operational Technology (OT), prompting the need for a specialized framework.

Method: The authors propose CritBench, which evaluates five state-of-the-art language models on 81 domain-specific tasks in OT environments, and design a tool scaffold for industrial protocol interaction.

Result: LLMs perform well in structured-file analysis and simple network tasks but struggle with dynamic tasks requiring sequential reasoning and live system interaction. The tool scaffold improves their performance.

Conclusion: While current LLMs demonstrate foundational knowledge of IEC 61850 standards, they require specialized tools to effectively operate in OT contexts. CritBench provides a pathway for further evaluation and improvement.

Abstract: The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has raised concerns regarding their dual-use potential in cybersecurity. Existing evaluation frameworks overwhelmingly focus on Information Technology (IT) environments, failing to capture the constraints, and specialized protocols of Operational Technology (OT). To address this gap, we introduce CritBench, a novel framework designed to evaluate the cybersecurity capabilities of LLM agents within IEC 61850 Digital Substation environments. We assess five state-of-the-art models, including OpenAI's GPT-5 suite and open-weight models, across a corpus of 81 domain-specific tasks spanning static configuration analysis, network traffic reconnaissance, and live virtual machine interaction. To facilitate industrial protocol interaction, we develop a domain-specific tool scaffold. Our empirical results show that agents reliably execute static structured-file analysis and single-tool network enumeration, but their performance degrades on dynamic tasks. Despite demonstrating explicit, internalized knowledge of the IEC 61850 standards terminology, current models struggle with the persistent sequential reasoning and state tracking required to manipulate live systems without specialized tools. Equipping agents with our domain-specific tool scaffold significantly mitigates this operational bottleneck. Code and evaluation scripts are available at: https://github.com/GKeppler/CritBench

</details>


### [531] [LLM4CodeRE: Generative AI for Code Decompilation Analysis and Reverse Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06095)
*Hamed Jelodar,Samita Bai,Tochukwu Emmanuel Nwankwo,Parisa Hamedi,Mohammad Meymani,Roozbeh Razavi-Far,Ali A. Ghorbani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces LLM4CodeRE, a domain-adaptive LLM framework tailored for bidirectional code reverse engineering, aiming at assembly-to-source and source-to-assembly translation, outperforming existing tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
